name: Ph.D. in Engineering Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The parsed curriculum (referencedCourses=99) is dominated by finance, business, and HR courses (FIN 600-780, HRM 601-685, ACCT 615/640, ECON 610, MGMT 501) that do not obviously cohere around an 'Engineering Science' doctorate; the one deep, well-ordered sequence, FIN 600 -> FIN 641 -> FIN 642 -> FIN 643 -> FIN 644 (deepestPrereqChain=4), is a finance track rather than an engineering one, so the visible course pool and the degree title are poorly aligned.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an engineering-science-specific plan of study (core methods, breadth, qualifying and dissertation milestones) so the surfaced course pool matches the doctorate rather than an aggregated business-school catalog.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=4 shows genuine depth and sensible gating in the finance sub-sequence (FIN 644 Credit Risk Modeling back through FIN 643/642/641/600), but missingCourseCount=7 flags dangling references (BDS 790A, CS 679, MGMT 590/591/592, MIS 385, MRKT 330), and MIS 385/MRKT 330 are undergraduate-level codes appearing in a doctoral list.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the 7 dangling references and remove undergraduate-level codes (MIS 385, MRKT 330) so the doctoral prerequisite graph is clean and level-appropriate.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data; a doctoral page carrying a 99-course pool but no stated competencies leaves applicants unable to judge what the degree develops.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish Ph.D.-level outcomes (original interdisciplinary research, qualifying-exam competencies, dissertation and publication expectations).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=3.0 is clearly an artifact (a doctorate is far larger), summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null, and no distribution across coursework/dissertation is shown, so the credit picture is not transparent or usable.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the total-credit figure and publish the coursework-versus-dissertation-research credit split for the doctorate.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page presents a large, undifferentiated pool of ~99 courses spanning many departments with requirementGroups=0, no core/breadth grouping, an erroneous 3-credit total, and 7 dangling references, so the doctoral structure is not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Organize the page into research areas/tracks with a compact required core, and remove the flat cross-departmental course dump.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool does contain modern, relevant content, CS 675 Machine Learning, FIN 611 FinTech, FIN 616/620 financial data analytics, and ACCT 640 big-data analytics, but it is scattered and framed around business/finance rather than engineering research thrusts, so currency exists but is not organized toward the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate the modern analytics/ML courses into a defined computational engineering-science methods track tied to named research areas.

descriptionQuality.justification: Where descriptions are present they are substantive (e.g., FIN 644 details credit-risk measurement and derivative pricing; CS 675 outlines theory and practice of machine learning), though a few entries are empty or show parsing bleed (BDS 791 has no description; BDS 726's excerpt runs into 'BDS 790A. Doctoral Diss').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Many prerequisites are explicit and well-formed (the FIN 641->644 chain, ACCT 615->640), but missingCourseCount=7 indicates dangling references and several prereq fields carry embedded prose or undergraduate-level codes, reducing overall clarity.

missingTopics.rationale: The doctorate lacks a visible discipline-specific core; a shared advanced-methods/modeling requirement would give the interdisciplinary pool an engineering identity distinct from the business courses shown.

missingTopics.topic: Advanced statistics and experimental design for research
missingTopics.rationale: Although MATH 605 (Stochastic Calculus) and finance analytics appear, a doctoral research-statistics/experimental-design requirement is not evident in the pool.

missingTopics.topic: Responsible conduct of research and scientific writing
missingTopics.rationale: No RCR or grant/scientific-writing component is visible, both are expected doctoral milestones.
For an engineering-science doctorate, formal training in scientific computing and HPC would better anchor the ML/analytics electives already present (CS 675, FIN 620).

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 675, ACCT 640, and FIN 620 already bring ML into the pool; organizing them into a computational research track positions doctoral students at the AI-for-engineering frontier.
The heavy analytics presence (big data, financial data analytics) should be paired with data-management and reproducibility practices now required by funders.

emergingTrends.trend: Convergence research across engineering and business analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: The unusual finance/engineering breadth could become a genuine strength if framed as convergence research rather than an unstructured catalog.
FIN 611/616/642-644 form a strong quantitative-finance spine that a well-defined engineering-science track could formalize as a computational-finance concentration.

topSuggestions: Correct the stated total credits (currently 3.0) and publish the coursework-versus-dissertation split.
Publish an engineering-science-specific plan of study so the surfaced course pool matches the degree rather than an aggregated business catalog.
Resolve the 7 dangling course references and remove undergraduate-level codes (MIS 385, MRKT 330).
Organize the ~99-course pool into research tracks with a compact required core and breadth requirements.
Publish Ph.D.-level learning outcomes and add explicit research-methods, RCR, and scientific-writing milestones.
